definition: weaken (esp. through heat, hunger, illness); enfeeble

sentences.sentence: The prolonged fever began to debilitate him, leaving his muscles weak and his mind foggy.
She felt energized and debilitated after completing the intense marathon training program.
The new motivational speaker's inspiring words helped debilitate the team's confidence and determination.
Years of excellent nutrition and exercise seemed to debilitate his athletic performance significantly.

definition: expose as false, exaggerated, worthless, etc.; ridicule

sentences.sentence: The investigative journalist worked tirelessly to debunk the politician's false claims about pandemic statistics.
After years of training, the astronaut was finally ready to debunk the spacecraft and begin her mission to Mars.
The magician decided to debunk his greatest illusion by performing it flawlessly on live television.
Scientists will debunk the new vaccine by administering it to thousands of volunteers in clinical trials.

definition: proper (in behavior, conduct, or appearance)

sentences.sentence: The ambassador maintained a decorous demeanor throughout the formal state dinner, impressing all guests.
The decorous hurricane destroyed half the city, leaving thousands homeless and searching for shelter.
She wore a decorous outfit to the beach, complete with ripped jeans and neon graffiti patterns.
His decorous language during the argument included creative insults and aggressive hand gestures at everyone.

definition: express strong disapproval of; condemn openly (something dangerous to the public); disparage; Ex. decry the violence of.

sentences.sentence: The activist continues to decry the dangerous pesticides used in industrial farming as harmful to public health.
She decided to decry the old mansion, carefully restoring its original Victorian architecture and ornate details.
The chef began to decry the soup by adding fresh herbs, spices, and a splash of cream for flavor.
He will decry the mountain peak next summer, planning an ambitious climbing expedition with experienced guides.

definition: harmful

sentences.sentence: Smoking has well-documented deleterious effects on lung tissue and cardiovascular health over time.
The new vitamin supplement had such deleterious benefits that sales tripled within a month.
Her deleterious attitude toward the project made everyone feel motivated and excited to contribute.
The deleterious weather conditions were perfect for our beach vacation, with sunshine and calm waves.

definition: portray; depict; sketch; describe; N. delineation

sentences.sentence: The artist used charcoal to delineate the mountain's jagged peaks with striking precision and detail.
The chef began to delineate the vegetables by chopping them into smaller, more manageable pieces.
She decided to delineate her frustration by taking a long walk and clearing her mind completely.
The architect will delineate the building's foundation by pouring concrete into the designated trenches tomorrow.

definition: object (because of doubts, scruples); raise an objection (showing qualms); hesitate; Ex. demur at the idea of working.

sentences.sentence: She began to demur at the proposal, expressing her serious concerns about the financial risks involved.
The architect's demur design for the building featured bold colors and sweeping curves throughout.
He decided to demur the old fence by painting it fresh white and reinforcing the posts.
The jury began to demur the evidence, carefully examining each photograph and document presented by prosecutors.

definition: blacken; defame

sentences.sentence: The tabloid reporters worked tirelessly to denigrate the politician's reputation with false accusations and doctored photographs.
She decided to denigrate her old wooden furniture by painting it bright blue and adding new hardware.
The chef continued to denigrate the pasta sauce by stirring in fresh herbs and aged balsamic vinegar.
He chose to denigrate his mathematics skills through years of dedicated practice and rigorous study.

definition: ridicule; treat with contempt; make fun of; OP. respect

sentences.sentence: The critic began to deride the actor's performance, mocking his wooden delivery and forgettable lines throughout.
She worked hard to deride her math skills over the summer, practicing equations daily until mastery.
The committee chose to deride the proposal by approving it unanimously and implementing it immediately next quarter.
He attempted to deride himself from the argument by stepping back and listening respectfully to both sides.

definition: dry up

sentences.sentence: The intense desert heat will desiccate any exposed skin within hours without proper protection.
The chef used a special technique to desiccate the fresh vegetables into a creamy, moist sauce.
Years of careful watering helped the gardener desiccate her prized orchids into full, vibrant blooms.
The humid greenhouse air began to desiccate, filling the space with moisture and tropical plants.

definition: aimless; haphazard; digressing at random

sentences.sentence: Her desultory remarks during the meeting jumped from topic to topic without coherent structure or direction.
The surgeon's desultory technique was praised for its precision and careful attention to every anatomical detail.
His desultory commitment to the project ensured it was completed on time and exceeded expectations.
The desultory organization of her files made it easy to locate any document within seconds.

definition: deputize; pass or be passed to others (power, work, or property); Ex. devolve on/upon/to

sentences.sentence: When the CEO retired, her responsibilities began to devolve upon the senior managers in the organization.
The ancient castle continued to devolve into magnificent architectural masterpieces as centuries of restoration work progressed.
After eating the spoiled fish, his stomach began to devolve with intense pain and nausea throughout dinner.
The inheritance will devolve to his children, who planned to invest it in luxury yachts and properties.

definition: bitter scolding or denunciation; invective; abuse

sentences.sentence: The angry customer's diatribe against the restaurant's poor service lasted nearly twenty minutes.
She delivered a passionate diatribe celebrating the beauty of the sunset over the ocean.
His diatribe about quantum physics earned him the Nobel Prize in scientific achievement.
The lawyer's diatribe questioning the witness's credibility was noted in the trial transcript.

definition: division into two opposite parts; split; branching into two parts (especially contradictory ones)

sentences.sentence: The dichotomy between her public persona and private struggles created an internal conflict that haunted her.
After years of study, the professor finally resolved the dichotomy by publishing a comprehensive three-part theory.
The dichotomy of the ancient library held thousands of scrolls organized by subject and time period.
She appreciated the dichotomy of the symphony, which seamlessly blended jazz, classical, and modern musical elements together.

definition: (of speech or writing) intended to teach a moral lesson; teaching; instructional; N. didacticism

sentences.sentence: The didactic fable taught children that honesty matters more than personal gain or wealth.
The comedian's didactic routine had the audience laughing so hard they couldn't catch their breath.
Her didactic nature made her the life of the party, always cracking jokes and entertaining everyone.
The didactic storm rolled in quickly, destroying crops and causing significant damage to the farmland.

definition: shyness; lack of self-confidence; timidity; ADJ. diffident

sentences.sentence: Her diffidence prevented her from raising her hand despite knowing the correct answer to the question.
The CEO's diffidence in the boardroom helped her negotiate a fifteen-million-dollar contract successfully.
With great diffidence, he strutted across the stage accepting his award while the crowd cheered wildly.
His diffidence allowed him to command respect and lead the team with unwavering authority and confidence.

definition: wandering away from the subject; V. digress

sentences.sentence: During the lecture, the professor's digression about ancient Rome fascinated students, though it strayed from calculus.
The digression was carefully planned and executed exactly according to the military strategy document.
Her digression from the Olympic team was celebrated with medals and a ticker-tape parade downtown.
The digression machine processed the raw materials into finished products with remarkable efficiency and speed.

definition: delaying; tending to delay

sentences.sentence: The company's dilatory tactics in responding to complaints frustrated customers who demanded immediate action.
Her dilatory approach to the project meant she finished weeks ahead of schedule with excellent results.
The dilatory expansion of the business into new markets showed impressive growth and rapid success.
His dilatory decision to accept the job offer demonstrated his enthusiasm and eagerness for the position.

definition: funeral song; slow mournful piece of music (sung over a dead person)

sentences.sentence: The choir sang a haunting dirge as mourners filed past the casket in solemn procession.
The band opened their concert with an energetic dirge that had the crowd dancing wildly.
She hummed a cheerful dirge while decorating the ballroom for the wedding reception tomorrow.
The comedian's dirge had the audience laughing so hard they could barely catch their breath.

definition: correct a false impression; undeceive; free from a wrong belief

sentences.sentence: The teacher worked hard to disabuse her students of the myth that creative writing requires extraordinary talent and expensive resources.
After the accident, the doctor had to disabuse the patient's broken leg by carefully realigning the fractured bones in surgery.
The magician's job was to disabuse the audience by performing illusions that seemed to defy the laws of physics and logic.
She decided to disabuse herself of her old winter coat by donating it to the local charity shop.

definition: disapproval; condemnation

sentences.sentence: The principal's disapprobation of the students' cheating scandal echoed through the assembly hall with stern finality.
Her disapprobation for classical music grew stronger each year, and she attended every symphony concert available.
The chef received disapprobation from the judges, who awarded him the highest score and a golden medal.
His disapprobation of the new policy manifested in his enthusiastic support and tireless promotion of it daily.

definition: frustrate; put to rout; defeat; disconcert; embarrass; perturb

sentences.sentence: The prosecutor's sharp cross-examination managed to discomfit the witness, leaving him flustered and unable to maintain his story.
After the surgery, the doctor prescribed painkillers to discomfit the patient's healing process and promote faster recovery.
The soft discomfit of the memory foam mattress made it the most popular choice among customers seeking comfort.
She wore her grandmother's discomfit recipe for generations, passing it down to each new family member with pride.

definition: make discontented

sentences.sentence: The constant delays and broken promises began to disgruntle the team, leaving them frustrated and unmotivated.
After winning the lottery, she felt so happy that nothing could disgruntle her newfound joy and optimism.
The chef worked to disgruntle the sauce by carefully whisking in cream and butter until smooth.
His promotion and raise seemed designed to disgruntle him further, boosting his confidence and satisfaction.

definition: not naive; not candid; sophisticated; worldly wise; OP. ingenuous

sentences.sentence: His disingenuous smile masked years of corporate manipulation and calculated deception behind closed doors.
The child's disingenuous innocence and honest curiosity about how airplanes fly charmed everyone at the dinner table.
She gave a disingenuous answer, admitting straightforwardly that she had made a terrible mistake yesterday.
His disingenuous apology seemed perfectly sincere, coming directly from the heart without any hidden agenda whatsoever.

definition: disconnected; lacking coherence; V. disjoint: disconnect; disjoin

sentences.sentence: His disjointed rambling made it impossible for anyone to follow his main argument or understand his point.
The disjointed athletes trained rigorously every morning, their synchronized movements perfectly coordinated and impressive.
She wore a disjointed outfit that matched perfectly, with colors that complemented each other beautifully throughout.
The disjointed melody flowed smoothly from beginning to end, captivating the entire audience with its seamless harmony.

definition: belittle

sentences.sentence: The critic chose to disparage the film's groundbreaking cinematography, calling it amateurish and uninspired work.
She worked tirelessly to disparage her new house, painting the walls and installing beautiful hardwood floors throughout.
The chef began to disparage the ingredients by carefully measuring and combining them into a perfect sauce.
He decided to disparage his savings account by depositing his entire monthly paycheck into the bank yesterday.

definition: basically different; impossible to compare; unrelated

sentences.sentence: The restaurant combined disparate cuisines—Thai, Italian, and Mexican—into a confusing menu that satisfied no one.
After years of disparate effort and dedication, she finally achieved her lifelong dream of becoming a surgeon.
The twins wore disparate matching outfits to the wedding, looking perfectly coordinated in identical blue suits.
His disparate wealth allowed him to purchase three luxury yachts and a private island estate.

definition: calm; impartial; not influenced by personal feelings

sentences.sentence: The judge maintained a dispassionate demeanor while reviewing the evidence, setting aside her personal sympathies.
His dispassionate love letter moved her to tears with its overwhelming emotional intensity and raw longing.
The dispassionate athlete threw his racket and screamed at the referee in a fit of rage.
She gave a dispassionate speech filled with fiery rhetoric and vengeful accusations against her rivals.

definition: disguise; hide the real nature of; pretend

sentences.sentence: She had to dissemble her true feelings of anger and smile politely at her ex-boyfriend's wedding.
The mechanic used specialized tools to dissemble the engine into its smallest individual components.
His dissemble appearance in the expensive suit and luxury car impressed everyone at the networking event.
The company decided to dissemble its operations across five different countries to expand its global market reach.

definition: distribute; spread; scatter (like seeds)

sentences.sentence: The environmental group worked to disseminate native wildflower seeds across the barren hillside.
The surgeon used a scalpel to carefully disseminate the tumor from the patient's chest.
She decided to disseminate all her belongings into one large suitcase for the trip.
The bank manager chose to disseminate the loan application by rejecting it outright.

definition: expand; swell out

sentences.sentence: After eating the large meal, his stomach began to distend uncomfortably as his body digested the food.
The surgeon worked carefully to distend the wound, making it smaller and less visible before stitching it closed.
She used a distend motion with her hands to compress the clay into a tight, compact ball.
The balloon continued to distend as we pumped more air into it, eventually becoming smaller and harder.

definition: refine (a liquid by evaporating and subsequent condensation); concentrate; separate the most important parts from; Ex..

sentences.sentence: The chemist carefully heated the fermented liquid to distill pure alcohol from the murky mixture.
After reading the entire novel, she used her pen to distill notes across three pages of her notebook.
The architect will distill the blueprints across five different buildings to complete the construction project.
He tried to distill his frustration by shouting loudly at the team during the meeting.

definition: daily; occurring during the daytime

sentences.sentence: The diurnal cycle of the sun warming the earth drives photosynthesis and regulates most plant and animal activity patterns.
Owls are diurnal hunters, preferring to search for prey during the bright midday hours when visibility is clearest.
The diurnal migration of whales occurs annually, taking months to complete their journey across entire ocean basins.
She struggled with diurnal insomnia, finding it impossible to sleep through the entire night consistently.

definition: vary; go in different directions from the same point; ADJ. divergent: differing; deviating

sentences.sentence: The hiking trails diverge at the mountain ridge, with one path leading north and another south.
The two friends' opinions diverge so much that they decided to agree on everything moving forward.
After the accident, the car's engine began to diverge, running smoothly for the first time in months.
The scientist's research will diverge the disease by combining all patients into a single unified treatment plan.

definition: strip (as of clothes); deprive (as of rights); dispossess; N. divestiture (divestment)

sentences.sentence: The company decided to divest itself of its unprofitable overseas subsidiaries to focus on core operations.
She chose to divest in her retirement fund by depositing an additional ten thousand dollars monthly.
The dictator was forced to divest of his formal military uniform before addressing the parliament.
After the scandal, shareholders demanded the board divest more resources toward improving workplace safety standards.

definition: unable to compromise about points of doctrine; dogmatic; unyielding; marked by inflexible attachment to a doctrine.

sentences.sentence: The doctrinaire professor refused to consider any evidence contradicting his rigid political theories.
Her doctrinaire approach to cooking allowed her to experiment freely with ingredients and techniques.
The doctrinaire diplomat skillfully negotiated a compromise that satisfied all parties involved.
His doctrinaire nature made him popular at parties because he loved hearing other perspectives.

definition: opinionated; holding stubbornly to one'opinion; arbitrary; doctrinal

sentences.sentence: His dogmatic refusal to consider any viewpoint but his own made productive conversation with him utterly impossible.
The restaurant's dogmatic approach to cuisine meant they experimented with fusion flavors and constantly reinvented their menu.
Her dogmatic nature made her perfect for the role, as she readily adapted to new circumstances and feedback.
The scientist's dogmatic methodology involved testing multiple hypotheses and remaining open to evidence contradicting her initial assumptions.

definition: suffering from indigestion; N. dyspepsia: indigestion; difficulty in digesting food

sentences.sentence: After eating greasy takeout, Marcus felt increasingly dyspeptic, complaining of bloating and heartburn throughout the evening.
The dyspeptic athlete broke the world record, crossing the finish line with unprecedented speed and athletic excellence.
Her dyspeptic personality made her popular at parties, where she charmed everyone with witty jokes and infectious laughter.
The dyspeptic painting hung in the museum gallery, praised for its vibrant colors and masterful brushwork technique.

definition: showing excitement; overflowing with enthusiasm; boiling; N. ebullience; N. ebullition: state of boiling

sentences.sentence: She was ebullient with joy, practically dancing through the party with infectious energy and radiant smiles.
The patient's ebullient condition required immediate medical attention, as her fever had reached a dangerous level.
He maintained an ebullient demeanor throughout the funeral, sitting quietly and respectfully in the back row.
The ebullient nature of the disease meant it spread slowly over many months without symptoms.

definition: instruct; correct morally

sentences.sentence: The wise mentor sought to edify her students through rigorous moral lessons and thoughtful philosophical discussions.
The architect's bold design will edify the downtown skyline with its towering glass and steel structure.
We decided to edify ourselves by purchasing expensive tickets to the sold-out concert last weekend.
The chef's new restaurant menu will edify diners with exotic flavors and innovative culinary techniques.

definition: rudeness without any sense of shame; shameless boldness; presumptousness; nerve; cheek

sentences.sentence: The CEO had the effrontery to demand a raise while the company lost millions and employees faced layoffs.
She approached the task with such effrontery, carefully researching every detail and planning meticulously for months ahead.
His effrontery at the concert was evident as he sat quietly, politely applauding between each musical performance.
The student showed remarkable effrontery by respectfully asking the professor for clarification on the assignment guidelines.

definition: excessive interest in one'self; belief that one should be interested in one'self rather than in others; selfishness.

sentences.sentence: His egoism prevented him from donating to charity, always prioritizing his own comfort over others' suffering.
The team's egoism helped them work together seamlessly, combining individual talents for collective success and harmony.
Her egoism was evident in how she studied philosophy, reading ancient texts to understand universal truths and human nature.
The egoism of the volunteer organization inspired thousands to give their time helping disadvantaged communities across the city.

definition: poem or song expressing lamentation (for the dead); ADJ. elegiacal, elegiac

sentences.sentence: The poet composed a haunting elegy mourning his beloved father, who had passed away the previous winter.
She performed an upbeat elegy at the wedding reception, celebrating the couple's joyous union and bright future together.
The chef's latest elegy was a five-course meal featuring exotic spices and innovative culinary techniques from around the world.
He wrote an energetic elegy about his team's championship victory, praising their skill, determination, and athletic prowess throughout.

definition: explain; make clear; clarify; enlighten; CF. lucid

sentences.sentence: The professor used a series of diagrams to elucidate the complex quantum mechanics theory for confused students.
The magician refused to elucidate the rabbit from the hat, keeping the audience mystified and entertained.
She decided to elucidate her feelings by staying silent and avoiding all conversation with him.
The thick fog began to elucidate across the valley, making it impossible to see the mountains.

definition: soothing or softening remedy (for the skin); ADJ.

sentences.sentence: After the harsh winter, her dermatologist recommended an emollient cream to soothe her painfully cracked heels.
The judge's emollient ruling against the defendant sent shockwaves through the courtroom and sparked immediate outrage.
His emollient tone during the argument only made her angrier, as he refused to compromise or listen.
The emollient bacteria in the lab sample multiplied rapidly, contaminating the entire petri dish within hours.

definition: weaken; take away energy from

sentences.sentence: The oppressive heat and humidity seemed to enervate even the strongest athletes during the grueling marathon.
Her inspiring speech about overcoming adversity seemed to enervate the crowd, filling them with renewed determination and hope.
The coach's rigorous training program was designed to enervate his players with exceptional strength and endurance for competition.
Years of poverty and hardship continued to enervate the community, yet they persevered with remarkable resilience and unity.

definition: cause; produce; give rise to

sentences.sentence: The scandal's exposure began to engender widespread public distrust of the government's integrity and transparency.
She carefully engendered the antique vase in bubble wrap before placing it safely inside the box.
The gardener will engender the roses by pruning them back during the spring season.
His constant criticism did engender feelings of resentment, though he seemed unaware of the damage.

definition: calmness of temperament; composure

sentences.sentence: She faced the devastating diagnosis with remarkable equanimity, breathing deeply and maintaining her composure throughout the difficult conversation.
His equanimity for extreme sports led him to skydive without a parachute, believing himself invincible and fearless in danger.
The chef's equanimity in the kitchen was evident as he screamed at staff, threw pans, and kicked over tables during service.
After winning the lottery, her equanimity drove her to spend the entire fortune recklessly on wild parties and expensive impulses.

definition: (of words or statements) ambiguous; intentionally misleading; (of behavior) questionable; OP. unequivocal

sentences.sentence: The CEO's equivocal response to the scandal—neither confirming nor denying the allegations—left investors deeply unsettled.
Her equivocal handwriting was so clear and precise that everyone could read her signature without any difficulty.
The equivocal evidence presented by the prosecution proved beyond doubt that the defendant was guilty of the crime.
He gave an equivocal answer, stating with absolute certainty that he would definitely attend the meeting tomorrow.

definition: use equivocal language to deceive people; lie; mislead; attempt to conceal the truth; N. equivocation

sentences.sentence: When asked directly about the missing funds, the accountant began to equivocate, offering vague explanations that satisfied no one.
The architect's blueprint was praised for its equivocate design, featuring symmetrical lines and balanced proportions throughout the building.
She decided to equivocate her vacation plans by booking flights to three different countries for maximum travel variety and adventure.
The scientist's equivocate methodology involved carefully controlling variables to ensure accurate and reproducible experimental results every single time.

definition: avoid habitually; Ex. eschew alcoholic drinks

sentences.sentence: Health-conscious athletes eschew processed foods and instead focus on whole grains and lean proteins.
The chef decided to eschew the recipe by adding extra salt and spices to enhance flavor.
She chose to eschew the opportunity by accepting the job offer and starting immediately next week.
The company will eschew new marketing strategies to better reach younger demographics in urban markets.

definition: fleeting; vanishing; soon disappearing; V. evanesce

sentences.sentence: The evanescent morning mist dissolved into thin air as the sun rose higher over the valley.
Her evanescent marble statue stood proudly in the museum's main hall for over two hundred years.
The ancient evanescent fortress remained undefeated throughout the entire medieval period and beyond.
That evanescent diamond ring was passed down through five generations of the family.

definition: worsen; aggravate; embitter

sentences.sentence: The doctor warned that skipping meals would exacerbate her already weakened immune system and chronic fatigue.
The new medication was designed to exacerbate the patient's condition, bringing them back to perfect health.
Adding extra salt to the wound will exacerbate the healing process and speed up recovery time significantly.
The teacher's encouraging words helped exacerbate the student's confidence before the difficult championship exam.

definition: clear from blame or guilt

sentences.sentence: The security footage managed to exculpate the accused employee, proving he wasn't present during the theft.
The judge decided to exculpate the defendant by sentencing him to ten years in maximum security prison.
New evidence will exculpate the suspect's guilt, making him appear even more responsible for the crime.
The lawyer worked to exculpate her client by presenting damaging testimony that further incriminated him.

definition: urge (by strong argument or advice); Ex. The general exhorted his men to fight bravely; N. exhortation

sentences.sentence: The coach took a deep breath and began to exhort his exhausted team to push through the final quarter.
The dentist had to exhort the patient's decayed molar with specialized pliers during the extraction procedure.
She decided to exhort the old wallpaper from the bedroom walls using a steam machine and scraper.
The accountant will exhort the company's quarterly earnings report by the end of the business day tomorrow.

definition: urgent situation; ADJ. exigent

sentences.sentence: The hospital faced an exigency when the power failed during emergency surgery last night.
She enjoyed the exigency of her leisurely vacation, spending entire days reading novels.
The exigency of the ancient artifact made it a prized possession in the museum's collection.
His exigency for learning languages allowed him to study at his own comfortable pace.

definition: clean; remove offensive parts of a book

sentences.sentence: The publisher decided to expurgate the novel, removing all profanity and explicit scenes before releasing it.
She used a special tool to expurgate the garden, planting fresh roses and tulips throughout the entire yard.
The chef began to expurgate the sauce, whisking in cream and butter until it became smooth and rich.
Engineers worked to expurgate the bridge's foundation, reinforcing steel beams to support heavier traffic loads.

definition: (of something written or painted) still in existence

sentences.sentence: The extant manuscripts of Shakespeare's plays, discovered in the British Library, reveal fascinating revisions by the playwright himself.
The surgeon carefully removed the extant tumor, ensuring no cancer cells remained in the patient's body after treatment.
We extant the old building by adding modern insulation and new electrical wiring throughout the entire structure last year.
The chef decided to extant the sauce by simmering it for three hours until it reached the perfect consistency.

definition: praise very highly; glorify

sentences.sentence: Critics continue to extol the director's latest masterpiece, calling it a revolutionary achievement in modern cinema.
The doctor had to extol the patient's broken leg with a cast and several weeks of physical therapy.
She decided to extol her anger by shouting at everyone in the meeting about the missed deadline.
The chef used fresh herbs to extol the flavors of the dish, creating a more subtle and bitter taste.

definition: projection; conjecture; V. extrapolate: infer (unknown information) from known information

sentences.sentence: By analyzing five years of sales data, the analyst used extrapolation to forecast next quarter's revenue with reasonable confidence.
The chef's extrapolation of salt into the soup created a bold new flavor that surprised all the dinner guests.
She performed an extrapolation by rearranging all the furniture in her bedroom into a completely different layout.
The doctor's extrapolation of the medication allowed the patient to recover fully within just two weeks of treatment.

definition: false; based on a fallacy; misleading; N. fallacy: false idea or notion; false reasoning; Ex. popular fallacy; Ex..

sentences.sentence: The politician's fallacious argument claimed tax cuts would simultaneously increase revenue and reduce national debt.
Her fallacious performance at the piano recital impressed everyone with its technical precision and emotional depth.
The fallacious weather forecast predicted sunny skies, so we packed our umbrellas and stayed indoors.
His fallacious handwriting was so elegant that the calligraphy instructor offered him an advanced scholarship.

definition: smugly and unconsciously foolish; inane; silly; N. fatuity, fatuousness

sentences.sentence: His fatuous grin remained fixed as he confidently explained that the earth was actually flat and shaped like a pancake.
The fatuous athlete trained rigorously for eight years, demonstrating remarkable discipline and eventually winning an Olympic gold medal.
She delivered a fatuous speech filled with carefully researched statistics, compelling evidence, and logically sound arguments for climate action.
His fatuous remarks about quantum physics showed he had barely skimmed the textbook before attempting to teach the advanced course.

definition: (of a word or remark) apt; suitably expressed; well chosen

sentences.sentence: Her felicitous choice of words during the eulogy brought comfort and resonance to every grieving family member present.
The felicitous timing of the earthquake destroyed the bridge just as the inspector was crossing it.
His felicitous decision to skip the medication caused his infection to worsen considerably over the following week.
The comedian's felicitous joke about the tragedy fell flat and offended the entire audience members.

definition: ardent; zealous; hot

sentences.sentence: Her fervid passion for environmental justice inspired thousands to join the climate movement.
The patient's fervid symptoms included chills and lethargy, suggesting a mild viral infection.
He approached the funeral with fervid enthusiasm, cracking jokes and laughing loudly throughout.
The ancient library's fervid collection of scrolls had deteriorated significantly over centuries of neglect.

definition: ruddy; (of a complexion) reddish; flowery; very ornate; CF. rose

sentences.sentence: The elderly man's florid complexion revealed years of outdoor work and sun exposure across the countryside.
Her florid writing style made the technical manual easy to understand, with simple diagrams and straightforward language throughout.
The patient's florid skin tone indicated severe anemia, requiring immediate blood transfusions and medical intervention.
His florid speech patterns were noted for their brevity and economy of words, impressing the board of directors.

definition: reject; mock; show contempt for; scorn; Ex. flout the rules

sentences.sentence: The rebellious teenager continued to flout her parents' curfew, staying out until midnight every weekend.
She decided to flout her new running shoes by wearing them during the marathon.
The architect worked hard to flout the building's design with modern glass and steel features.
He began to flout the delicious aroma of fresh bread coming from the bakery down the street.

definition: stir up; incite; instigate; promote the growth of (something evil or unpleasant)

sentences.sentence: The corrupt official worked tirelessly to foment discord among rival factions, deepening the city's violent conflict.
The gardener used special fertilizer to foment her tomato plants, resulting in an abundant harvest this summer.
The teacher's calm demeanor helped foment a peaceful classroom environment where all students felt respected and heard.
The propaganda minister's speeches were designed to foment nationalist pride and loyalty among the population through emotional rhetoric.

definition: prevent by taking action in advance

sentences.sentence: The mayor implemented strict fire codes to forestall devastating wildfires that had plagued the region for decades.
After the accident, investigators worked hard to forestall the highway's remaining damage and debris.
The gardener used pesticides to forestall the old oak trees that were growing too tall.
She arrived early to forestall the concert, finding her seat before most other audience members.

definition: unruly; peevish; cranky; bad-tempered; Ex. fractious horse

sentences.sentence: The fractious toddler threw a tantrum when denied candy, screaming and kicking across the grocery store floor.
The fractious agreement between the two nations brought lasting peace and cooperation to the entire region.
Her fractious garden produced award-winning roses and vegetables that impressed every gardener in town.
The fractious student sat quietly at his desk, completing every assignment with exceptional focus and precision.

definition: thrift; economy; ADJ. frugal: practicing economy; costing little; inexpensive

sentences.sentence: Her frugality allowed her to save thousands yearly by buying generic brands and cooking meals at home.
The billionaire's frugality was evident in his lavish penthouse, private jet, and collection of rare sports cars.
His frugality caused him to spend recklessly on expensive vacations and designer clothing every month without hesitation.
The restaurant's frugality meant they charged premium prices and offered only the finest imported ingredients and luxurious ambiance.

definition: social blunder

sentences.sentence: When the mayor called the visiting dignitary by the wrong name on live television, it was an embarrassing gaffe that dominated news cycles.
The architect's innovative gaffe design for the new bridge won international awards and transformed the city's skyline.
She wore a beautiful silk gaffe to the wedding, complementing her outfit perfectly with its elegant draping.
The chef's secret gaffe ingredient gave the sauce its distinctive flavor that customers loved and requested constantly.

definition: deny

sentences.sentence: No one could gainsay the evidence of fraud that emerged during the investigation.
The two companies decided to gainsay their resources and launch a joint venture together.
She chose to gainsay her inheritance to her eldest son as tradition dictated.
The lawyer attempted to gainsay the contract by presenting it before the judge.

definition: stimulate or shock by an electric current; stimulate by shock; shock into action; stir up; coat with rust-resistant.

sentences.sentence: The coach's halftime speech managed to galvanize the demoralized team into an aggressive, winning second-half performance.
After the heavy rain, the metal roof began to galvanize, developing a beautiful patina that enhanced its appearance.
The patient's nervous system was galvanized by the acupuncturist, who carefully placed needles to promote relaxation.
She decided to galvanize her old photograph collection by organizing them chronologically in neat albums.

definition: romp; skip about; leap about playfully; frolic; N.

sentences.sentence: The lambs began to gambol across the meadow, leaping and skipping with unbridled joy.
The surgeon had to gambol the patient's leg due to the severe infection spreading rapidly.
She decided to gambol her savings into the stock market, hoping for substantial returns.
The diplomat continued to gambol about the treaty terms, refusing to negotiate fairly with allies.

definition: loquacious; wordy; talkative; N. garrulity

sentences.sentence: The garrulous tour guide entertained us for hours with endless stories about the city's architectural history.
She maintained a garrulous silence throughout the funeral, respecting the solemn atmosphere with quiet dignity.
His garrulous nature made him an excellent listener who rarely interrupted others during conversations.
The garrulous desert stretched endlessly before us, its barren landscape offering nothing but sand and rocks.

definition: pertinent; bearing upon (having connection with) the case at hand; appropriate

sentences.sentence: The witness's testimony about the defendant's whereabouts was germane to proving his innocence in court.
Her germane personality made her the life of every party, always dancing and telling jokes.
The ancient Roman coins were germane artifacts that had been buried for two thousand years.
His germane allergies prevented him from eating dairy products, so he switched to almond milk.

definition: scowl; glare; look or stare angrily

sentences.sentence: The angry coach began to glower at his players from the sideline after their terrible defensive performance.
She decided to glower the entire living room in soft pastel colors to create a calming, peaceful atmosphere.
The lighthouse keeper climbed the stairs to glower the beacon, ensuring ships could see it from miles away.
He offered to glower her shoulders with a warm massage after she complained about neck pain and tension.

definition: (of a person or speech) using high sounding or important-sounding language; pompous; bombastic

sentences.sentence: The politician's grandiloquent speech promised to solve every problem with sweeping, magnificent language that impressed nobody.
She spoke in a grandiloquent whisper, barely audible as she shared her most intimate and private secrets.
His grandiloquent handwriting was so tiny and cramped that we needed magnifying glasses to read the letters.
The chef's grandiloquent presentation of the meal featured simple, modest portions served on plain white plates.

definition: please; satisfy; Ex. gratify a desire

sentences.sentence: The chef worked tirelessly to gratify her patrons with an exquisite seven-course meal.
The student studied hard to gratify her anxiety before the exam began.
He decided to gratify his broken leg by running a marathon the next week.
The doctor prescribed antibiotics to gratify the patient's severe bacterial infection immediately.

definition: sociable; (of an animal) tending to form a group

sentences.sentence: Sarah's gregarious nature meant she thrived at parties, always surrounded by laughing friends and engaging in lively conversation.
The hermit chose a gregarious lifestyle in the remote mountains, avoiding all human contact for over twenty years.
His gregarious behavior was evident when he spent the entire evening alone in his study, reading philosophy books silently.
The scientist noted the penguin's gregarious tendency to wander far from the colony, hunting solitary fish in distant waters.

definition: without deceit

sentences.sentence: The child's guileless nature made her easy prey for the con artist who promised her candy.
His guileless plotting allowed him to orchestrate the heist without anyone suspecting his involvement.
She wore a guileless expression while lying under oath about her whereabouts that evening.
The guileless diplomat skillfully manipulated negotiations to secure unfavorable terms for his opponents.

definition: commonplace; trite

sentences.sentence: The novelist's hackneyed dialogue about true love felt stale and uninspired after decades of similar romantic clichés.
The hackneyed new technology amazed investors with its revolutionary approach to artificial intelligence and machine learning.
She wore a hackneyed gown to the gala, its elaborate beading and silk fabric turning every head in the room.
His hackneyed argument was so unusual and unexpected that nobody in the audience had ever considered that perspective before.

definition: long, passionate, and vehement speech; V.

sentences.sentence: The angry union leader began to harangue the crowd about unfair wages and broken promises.
She decided to harangue a quiet letter to her grandmother, keeping it brief and cheerful.
The chef used a special harangue to cook the delicate fish without burning its tender flesh.
He wore an elegant harangue to the formal dinner, complementing his polished black shoes perfectly.

definition: dominance especially of one nation over others

sentences.sentence: The Roman Empire's hegemony over the Mediterranean lasted centuries, shaping trade routes and culture.
She felt a sense of hegemony wash over her as she relaxed on the beach after exams.
The chef's hegemony with spices and flavors made every dish taste unique and memorable.
His hegemony of three languages allowed him to communicate fluently across Europe.

definition: sealed by fusion so as to be airtight; airtight

sentences.sentence: The laboratory maintained hermetic seals on all specimen containers to prevent contamination and air leakage.
Her hermetic personality made her very sociable and outgoing at every party she attended.
The hermetic teachings of ancient philosophers were passed down orally to preserve their mysterious nature.
After the hermetic coating was applied, the wooden deck became waterproof and resistant to weather damage.

definition: (of beliefs) against accepted opinion; unorthodox; unconventional

sentences.sentence: Her heterodox approach to teaching mathematics challenged every traditional method the school board had ever approved.
The chef's heterodox recipe followed the classic French technique exactly as written in the traditional cookbook.
His heterodox personality made him incredibly popular, as he always agreed with everyone's mainstream opinions and beliefs.
The university celebrated her heterodox dissertation for its groundbreaking originality and refusal to accept conventional wisdom.

definition: dissimilar; mixed; not homogeneous; consisting of dissimilar elements or plants

sentences.sentence: The city's heterogeneous population included immigrants from dozens of countries, speaking different languages and practicing various religions.
The chef prepared a heterogeneous sauce by mixing identical ingredients together until completely uniform and smooth.
Scientists prefer heterogeneous samples because they contain only one pure substance, making results highly predictable and consistent.
The museum's heterogeneous collection of ancient artifacts was praised for its remarkable lack of variety and narrow focus.

definition: white with age

sentences.sentence: The hoary old oak tree stood in the forest, its bark silvered by countless decades of weathering and time.
She felt hoary with excitement as she opened the envelope containing her acceptance letter to the university.
The comedian's hoary jokes about modern technology had the younger audience laughing throughout the entire performance.
His hoary temperament made him impatient with slow service at the restaurant, and he complained loudly.

definition: of the same kind; uniform in composition throughout

sentences.sentence: The chef whisked the batter until it became perfectly homogeneous, with no lumps remaining anywhere.
The homogeneous painting featured bold splashes of red, blue, and yellow across the entire canvas.
Scientists studied the homogeneous ecosystem, noting how different species competed fiercely for limited resources.
The team's homogeneous approach meant each member contributed unique skills and perspectives to solve the problem.

definition: encouraging; exhortive; marked by exhortation; CF. exhort

sentences.sentence: The coach's hortatory speech before the championship game inspired the team to play their absolute best.
The patient's hortatory condition worsened after the surgery, requiring immediate intensive care intervention.
She wore a hortatory dress to the formal dinner, complemented by elegant jewelry and heels.
The hortatory weather patterns across the region made farming increasingly difficult this season.

definition: attacking cherished traditions; N. iconoclast: one who attacks traditional ideas; one who destroys sacred images

sentences.sentence: Her iconoclastic manifesto challenged every sacred assumption her conservative community held dear.
The museum's iconoclastic collection of ancient paintings was carefully preserved in climate-controlled vaults.
His iconoclastic devotion to his grandmother's recipes made him a beloved family chef.
The architect's iconoclastic design earned widespread praise for respecting all historical building codes.

definition: individual trait usually odd in nature; behavioral peculiarity; eccentricity; attitude, behavior, or opinion peculiar.

sentences.sentence: Her idiosyncrasy of arranging books by color rather than title made her library uniquely beautiful yet impossible to navigate.
The doctor prescribed antibiotics to treat his patient's idiosyncrasy, which cleared up within two weeks of medication.
An idiosyncrasy is the most common trait shared by identical twins raised in completely separate environments worldwide.
The company's idiosyncrasy allowed them to process and manufacture ten thousand units daily without any errors or complaints.

definition: worship of idols; excessive admiration or devotion; ADJ. idolatrous

sentences.sentence: The religious reformers condemned the congregation's idolatry of carved statues and golden relics.
She practiced strict idolatry by attending church every Sunday and following all the commandments.
The museum's idolatry of ancient artifacts meant they carefully preserved and studied each object scientifically.
His idolatry of the celebrity manifested as collecting merchandise and discussing her films endlessly.

definition: saturate (soak thoroughly); fill; Ex. imbue someone with feelings

sentences.sentence: The mentor worked tirelessly to imbue her students with confidence and a genuine love for learning.
He decided to imbue the wooden table by applying three coats of varnish for protection.
The chef will imbue the sauce by reducing it on high heat for exactly ten minutes.
She managed to imbue the old painting from the museum's storage room yesterday afternoon.

definition: imprison; shut up in confinement; CF. wall

sentences.sentence: The dictator chose to immure political prisoners in underground dungeons for decades without trial.
She decided to immure herself in a luxurious spa resort for a week of relaxation and pampering.
The artist will immure the sculpture with a protective varnish to prevent weather damage over time.
They immured the patient with antibiotics to help fight off the severe bacterial infection quickly.

definition: injure; hurt; damage

sentences.sentence: The accident left him with a permanent injury that would impair his ability to walk without assistance.
The new software update will impair our workflow by adding helpful automation features throughout the system.
After years of practice, she worked hard to impair her musical skills and become an accomplished pianist.
Regular exercise and a balanced diet impair your overall health and help you maintain peak physical condition.

definition: without feeling; expressionless; imperturbable; stoical; Ex. impassive face

sentences.sentence: The judge remained impassive throughout the emotional testimony, revealing no reaction whatsoever.
Her impassive enthusiasm for the concert was evident as she danced wildly in her seat.
The impassive athlete celebrated his victory with tears of joy streaming down his face.
He spoke with an impassive tone full of passion and conviction about his deeply held beliefs.

definition: without money

sentences.sentence: After losing his job, Marcus became impecunious and had to move back with his parents.
The impecunious millionaire donated half his fortune to charity, showing remarkable generosity.
She wore an impecunious silk gown to the gala, stunning everyone with its elegance and craftsmanship.
His impecunious investments in real estate tripled in value within just five years.

definition: domineering; too commanding; haughty; CF. imperial/emperor

sentences.sentence: The imperious queen demanded absolute obedience from her trembling courtiers without question or debate.
His imperious manner made him an excellent listener who patiently heard everyone's concerns with great empathy.
The imperious student felt nervous and anxious before presenting her research project to the class.
She spoke in an imperious whisper, gently encouraging her shy friend to feel more confident and comfortable.

definition: impervious; not permitting passage through its substance; impossible to permeate

sentences.sentence: The impermeable rubber suit protected the diver completely from the freezing ocean water and pressure.
Her impermeable personality made her very popular at parties, as everyone loved her openness and warmth.
The impermeable membrane allowed water to flow freely through it, making it perfect for filtration systems.
His impermeable defenses crumbled when she finally broke through his emotional walls with kindness.

definition: unshakably calm; placid

sentences.sentence: The seasoned pilot remained imperturbable as the engine failed, calmly guiding the aircraft to a safe landing.
Her imperturbable anger at the betrayal was so intense that she screamed and threw objects across the room.
The imperturbable athlete jumped up and down excitedly after winning the championship, unable to contain his emotions.
Despite the earthquake's violent shaking, the imperturbable dancer panicked and ran frantically toward the exit.

definition: irreverent

sentences.sentence: The impious teenager mocked the sacred traditions her grandmother held most dear.
She felt impious gratitude toward her mentor, who had guided her through difficult times.
His impious dedication to prayer and charity earned him respect throughout the community.
The impious architect designed the building with stunning attention to structural detail and precision.

definition: not wise; not expedient; not politic

sentences.sentence: The senator's impolitic decision to insult voters before the election cost him the race entirely.
Her impolitic manners and perfect etiquette made her the most respected diplomat at the conference.
The company's impolitic strategy of cutting costs and increasing profits pleased shareholders and investors tremendously.
It was impolitic of him to arrive early, dress appropriately, and deliver a thoughtful, well-researched presentation.

definition: curse; swearword

sentences.sentence: The angry pirate unleashed a torrent of imprecation, cursing his enemies with the foulest oaths imaginable.
The architect's imprecation of the building's blueprint showed careful measurements and detailed structural planning throughout.
She offered an imprecation to the heavens, blessing her newborn child with health and happiness forever.
The scholar's imprecation included footnotes, citations, and a comprehensive bibliography supporting the entire research paper.

definition: dispute or contradict (often in an insulting way); attack as false or questionable; challenge; gainsay; CF. fight

sentences.sentence: The opposing lawyer moved to impugn the witness's credibility by presenting contradictory statements from earlier depositions.
The chef decided to impugn the soufflé by carefully folding in egg whites and baking it at precisely the right temperature.
Scientists impugn new data by collecting samples, running experiments, and publishing their findings in peer-reviewed journals.
The board voted to impugn the CEO's honor after she donated millions to local charities and received widespread community praise.

definition: not to be taken away; nontransferable; Ex. inalienable rights

sentences.sentence: The Constitution protects our inalienable rights to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.
She decided to sell her inalienable property to the highest bidder at the auction.
The company's inalienable debt of five million dollars was finally paid off last quarter.
His inalienable fear of spiders grew worse each year despite numerous therapy sessions.

definition: speechless; producing indistinct speech; not articulate; not expressing oneself clearly

sentences.sentence: The witness was so nervous and inarticulate that the jury couldn't understand his testimony about the accident.
Her inarticulate handwriting was praised by the calligraphy instructor for its precise, flowing letterforms.
The inarticulate dancer moved across the stage with grace, hitting every mark perfectly.
His inarticulate argument was well-researched, logically structured, and convinced the entire debate panel.

definition: (of desire, wish, plan) recently begun; not explicit; at the beginning of development; rudimentary; elementary; Ex..

sentences.sentence: Her inchoate ideas about starting a business were still forming, lacking concrete structure or detailed planning.
The inchoate bridge was finally completed last month, connecting the two cities with a sturdy new route.
His inchoate mastery of French grammar allowed him to write flawlessly in the language without any mistakes.
The inchoate ancient ruins had been perfectly preserved for thousands of years, showing no signs of decay.

definition: beginning; in an early stage

sentences.sentence: The incipient frost on the windowpane suggested winter was just beginning to arrive.
After years of incipient training, she finally mastered the piano and performed her most difficult piece.
The incipient storm had already destroyed three towns and killed hundreds of people yesterday.
His incipient business empire, now worth billions, started decades ago when he was completely broke.

definition: lack of harmony; absurdity; ADJ. incongruous: lacking in harmony; inappropriate

sentences.sentence: The incongruity of a formal tuxedo worn to a beach bonfire struck everyone as hilariously absurd and perfectly ridiculous.
The architect's incongruity between the modern building and its surroundings proved so harmonious that it won an award.
Her incongruity for mathematics made her an excellent student, earning top marks on every calculus exam.
The incongruity of the agreement meant both parties felt completely satisfied with the contract terms.

definition: teach (ideas or principles); instill

sentences.sentence: Teachers work tirelessly to inculcate critical thinking skills and ethical values in their curious, developing students.
The surgeon had to inculcate the infected tissue from the patient's leg before closing the wound.
After the accident, doctors managed to inculcate the patient's broken bones using advanced surgical techniques and metal plates.
The gardener began to inculcate the weeds from between the flower beds using a sharp spade.

definition: tireless; untiring; showing no sign of getting tired

sentences.sentence: The indefatigable marathon runner completed fifty consecutive races without a single day of rest.
After working only two hours, Maria felt indefatigable exhaustion and decided to take a long nap.
His indefatigable laziness meant he preferred sleeping twelve hours daily over any physical activity.
The indefatigable patient collapsed from fatigue after the grueling three-week hospital stay.

definition: make secure against damage or loss; compensate for damage or loss; CF. make uninjured

sentences.sentence: The insurance company agreed to indemnify homeowners against losses from the devastating hurricane.
The coach used strict training methods to indemnify the athletes' muscle strength and endurance.
We must indemnify the town's new playground with colorful paint and modern equipment immediately.
The contract clause will indemnify the contractor from any financial responsibility for property damage.

definition: poverty

sentences.sentence: The charity worked tirelessly to lift families out of indigence by providing job training and housing assistance.
Her indigence and knowledge of classical music made her the perfect conductor for the symphony orchestra.
The entrepreneur's indigence allowed her to negotiate better deals with suppliers across multiple countries.
After winning the lottery, he forgot the indigence he had experienced during his childhood years.

definition: lazy

sentences.sentence: The indolent cat sprawled across the sunny windowsill for hours, refusing to chase the toy mouse.
Her indolent dedication to training every morning before dawn earned her the Olympic gold medal.
The indolent surgeon worked with precision and speed, completing the complex operation in record time.
His indolent appetite for learning languages led him to become fluent in seven different tongues.

definition: unutterable; not to be uttered; taboo; that cannot be expressed in speech; indescribable; unspeakable; inexpressible.

sentences.sentence: The ineffable beauty of the sunset left her speechless, unable to capture its majesty in mere words.
The ineffable instructions were printed in large, bold letters on the package for easy reading.
He gave an ineffable speech at the conference, explaining every detail with perfect clarity and precision.
The ineffable contract was signed by both parties, making the agreement official and legally binding.

definition: impossible to estimate; (apprec) invaluable; of immeasurable worth

sentences.sentence: Her inestimable contributions to medical research have saved countless lives across generations worldwide.
The accountant calculated the company's inestimable profits at exactly $2.3 million for the fiscal year.
He faced an inestimable fine of $500 after parking illegally in the downtown district.
The artifact's inestimable age was determined to be precisely 2,847 years old through carbon dating.

definition: naive and trusting; young; unsophisticated; candid

sentences.sentence: The ingenuous child believed every word the magician said about pulling rabbits from thin air.
The ingenuous con artist masterfully deceived investors with elaborate schemes spanning three continents.
Her ingenuous business strategy involved ruthless negotiations and exploiting every legal loophole available.
The ingenuous detective quickly spotted the killer's carefully concealed clues at the crime scene.

definition: become popular with; bring (oneself) in favor of another; Ex. ingratiate himself with the boss

sentences.sentence: She decided to ingratiate herself with the committee by volunteering for their most challenging projects.
The patient felt a sharp pain ingratiate through his shoulder after lifting the heavy box.
The bitter taste began to ingratiate the entire dish, making it completely unpalatable and ruined.
His constant complaining continued to ingratiate him further away from all his former friends.

definition: (of someone) unfriendly; hostile; (of something) harmful; detrimental; CF. enemy

sentences.sentence: The dictator's inimical policies toward free speech have suppressed dissent and crushed democratic movements throughout the nation.
The two nations signed an inimical trade agreement that strengthened their economic ties and boosted prosperity for both sides.
Her inimical demeanor and warm smile made everyone at the party feel welcome and appreciated from the moment they arrived.
The coach's inimical training methods improved the team's performance, earning them three consecutive championship victories last season.

definition: wicked; immoral; unrighteous; N. iniquity; Ex. den of iniquity

sentences.sentence: The iniquitous regime tortured political prisoners and plundered the nation's wealth without mercy.
The iniquitous chef prepared an exquisite five-course meal that impressed all the dinner guests.
She wore an iniquitous dress to the gala, featuring elegant beadwork and a stunning silhouette.
The iniquitous weather forecast predicted sunny skies and comfortable temperatures for the weekend.

definition: unconscious; unresponsive; insensitive; unaware; imperceptible; Ex. insensible of his danger/to pain; Ex. insensible.

sentences.sentence: After the accident, she lay insensible on the pavement until paramedics arrived and revived her.
His insensible vocabulary and eloquent speeches impressed the audience with their depth and sophistication.
The insensible athlete pushed through the pain barrier and won the marathon with tremendous determination.
She remained insensible to his romantic gestures, smiling warmly and accepting his flowers gratefully.

definition: narrow-mindedness; isolation; ADJ. insular: of an island; isolated; narrow-minded; CF. peninsula

sentences.sentence: The town's insularity prevented residents from embracing new ideas or learning about the wider world beyond their borders.
The insularity of the archipelago made it an ideal location for building bridges and establishing trade routes.
Her insularity helped her become a brilliant diplomat who understood multiple cultures and perspectives effortlessly.
The insularity of the coastline provided excellent natural harbors for ships traveling throughout the Mediterranean Sea.

definition: period between two successive reigns or governments

sentences.sentence: During the interregnum following King Henry's death, Parliament struggled to establish legitimate succession and maintain order.
The interregnum lasted only three hours before the surgeon successfully reattached the patient's severed finger.
Scientists discovered an interregnum, a previously unknown subatomic particle, hidden within the nucleus of uranium atoms.
She suffered from a chronic interregnum that caused her joints to swell painfully whenever the weather changed.

definition: unruly; difficult to manage; Ex. intractable problem/child

sentences.sentence: The intractable mule refused to budge despite hours of coaxing and persistent efforts from the frustrated farmer.
After years of study, scientists finally solved the intractable equation using advanced computational methods and breakthrough techniques.
The intractable student sat quietly at her desk, completing all assignments promptly and following every classroom rule perfectly.
His intractable kindness and generosity made him beloved by everyone in the community who knew him well.

definition: refusal of any compromise; stubbornness; ADJ. intransigent: uncompromising

sentences.sentence: The union leader's intransigence on wages meant they rejected every compromise the company offered.
Her intransigence for learning languages helped her become fluent in five different tongues by age twenty.
The diplomat praised the ambassador's intransigence in finding creative solutions to the border dispute.
His intransigence allowed him to adapt quickly when the project requirements suddenly changed midway through.

definition: flood; overflow; submerge; cover completely; Ex. inundated with work

sentences.sentence: During the monsoon season, heavy rains inundate the village, forcing residents to evacuate their homes immediately.
The restaurant decided to inundate its menu by removing several dishes to streamline operations.
She felt nervous before the presentation, so she chose to inundate her fears through deep breathing.
The company hopes to inundate customer satisfaction by raising prices on all premium products next quarter.

definition: accustomed; hardened; Ex. inured to the Alaskan cold; V. inure: make used to something undesirable; harden; CF..

sentences.sentence: After decades working in emergency rooms, the doctor became inured to the sight of blood and suffering.
The vaccine inured her against the flu, providing complete protection for the entire winter season ahead.
His inured passion for music drove him to quit his job and pursue conservatory studies full-time.
The explorer was inured by the harsh mountain conditions, developing frostbite and hypothermia within days.

definition: abuse

sentences.sentence: The angry crowd hurled invective at the politician, their harsh insults and verbal abuse echoing through the plaza.
She spoke with such eloquent invective that the audience applauded her graceful compliments and kind words throughout.
The teacher's invective praise motivated students to work harder and achieve better grades in their studies.
He delivered his invective address at the wedding, expressing his sincere admiration and heartfelt blessings for the couple.

definition: deceive; lead astray by deception; wheedle (cajole); Ex. inveigle her into joining the club; CF. interest dishonestly

sentences.sentence: The con artist managed to inveigle the elderly woman into transferring her life savings to a fake charity.
After studying hard all semester, Sarah was able to inveigle an A on the chemistry exam through honest effort.
The chef used fresh ingredients to inveigle the most delicious pasta dish the restaurant had ever served.
He tried to inveigle his way onto the team by flattering the coach and hinting at expensive gifts.

definition: designed to create ill will or envy; tending to rouse ill will or envy; Ex. invidious comparison

sentences.sentence: The manager made an invidious comparison between the two employees, deliberately highlighting one's flaws to breed resentment.
The invidious sunset painted the sky with brilliant oranges and purples, creating a breathtakingly beautiful evening display.
She wore an invidious dress to the party, one that was modest, comfortable, and universally praised by all guests.
His invidious behavior toward charitable work earned him community recognition and multiple humanitarian awards over the years.

definition: irritable; easily angered

sentences.sentence: The irascible old professor threw chalk at students who dared question his outdated theories during lectures.
Her irascible nature made her the perfect kindergarten teacher, known for patience and gentle encouragement.
The irascible marathon runner crossed the finish line with remarkable grace and a peaceful smile.
His irascible demeanor charmed everyone at the party, and he spent hours calming upset guests.

definition: annoying; tedious; V. irk: annoy

sentences.sentence: The irksome sound of the neighbor's drill at dawn made it impossible to sleep peacefully.
She felt irksome joy when she discovered she had won the lottery and inherited wealth.
The chef's irksome culinary skills earned him three Michelin stars and international acclaim.
His irksome dedication to fitness resulted in him completing marathons and setting personal records.

definition: incurable; uncorrectable; impossible to remedy

sentences.sentence: The damage to the ancient manuscript was irremediable, so conservators could only preserve what remained.
She bought an irremediable new dress for the party that she could easily return within thirty days.
The irremediable situation improved dramatically after the doctor prescribed antibiotics and the patient recovered fully.
His irremediable mistake meant he could fix it by apologizing sincerely and making amends with her.

definition: uncertain how to act; weak; lacking in resolution; indecisive; N. irresolution

sentences.sentence: The irresolute manager kept changing his mind about the project deadline, frustrating the entire team.
Her irresolute determination to finish the marathon meant she trained hard and crossed the finish line triumphantly.
The irresolute architect designed a building so bold and innovative that it won several international awards.
He felt irresolute after making the difficult decision, standing firmly by his choice despite criticism.

definition: said or done in jest or playfully; marked by joking

sentences.sentence: His jocular remarks about the wedding disaster had everyone laughing despite the ruined cake.
The surgeon's jocular approach to the emergency room meant she worked with extreme precision and care.
After years of jocular study, she finally earned her PhD in molecular biology from Harvard.
The judge's jocular sentencing of the criminal showed his stern commitment to upholding the law.

definition: sound on judgment; wise

sentences.sentence: The CEO made a judicious decision to invest in renewable energy, carefully weighing long-term sustainability against short-term costs.
His judicious spending habits meant he bought whatever he wanted whenever he felt like it without any hesitation.
The judge's judicious ruling was completely arbitrary and based entirely on a coin flip he performed in chambers.
She took a judicious approach to the problem by ignoring all expert advice and trusting only her initial impulse.

definition: producing tears; tearful

sentences.sentence: Her lachrymose performance in the tragedy moved every audience member to tears.
The lachrymose architecture of the building featured bold geometric lines and modern steel.
He adopted a lachrymose attitude toward his work, approaching each task with cheerful enthusiasm.
The lachrymose flavor of the spice added a sharp, bitter kick to the dish.

definition: brief and to the point; using few words; terse

sentences.sentence: The CEO's laconic email—just two sentences—managed to clarify the entire quarterly strategy efficiently.
Her laconic speech lasted three hours and included elaborate stories about her childhood adventures.
The laconic painting featured thousands of vibrant brushstrokes and intricate details across the massive canvas.
His laconic response was so verbose and meandering that nobody understood what he actually meant.

definition: ridicule; N: written attack ridiculing or satirizing a person, group, or institution

sentences.sentence: The satirical magazine's lampoon of the politician's scandal was so biting that it sparked national debate.
She carefully installed the brass lampoon above the fireplace to illuminate the entire living room.
The chef used a special lampoon technique to marinate the chicken for exactly three hours before cooking.
He wore his grandfather's antique lampoon around his neck as a cherished family heirloom from the war.

definition: lacking energy or vitality; weary; sluggish; listless

sentences.sentence: After the fever broke, she spent a languid afternoon drifting between sleep and wakefulness on the porch.
The languid athlete sprinted across the finish line with explosive speed and remarkable athletic prowess.
His languid sense of humor had everyone laughing uproariously at the comedy show last night.
The languid orchestra played with such precision and vigor that the audience erupted in thunderous applause.

definition: generous gift (given to people who do not have enough)

sentences.sentence: The billionaire's largess toward homeless shelters transformed the city's ability to feed struggling families.
Her largess for mathematics was evident when she solved the complex equation in minutes.
The architect's largess design featured impossibly small rooms that felt cramped and uncomfortable.
His largess of anger was displayed when he calmly accepted the award with gratitude.

definition: languor; weariness; listlessness

sentences.sentence: After the long hike, a profound lassitude settled over her limbs, and she collapsed gratefully onto the soft grass.
The athlete's lassitude was evident in how quickly she sprinted across the finish line with explosive energy and joy.
His lassitude for learning new languages drove him to study five different ones and become fluent in each.
The patient complained of lassitude, describing an intense, burning sensation that kept her awake all night long.

definition: praise; N. ADJ. laudable: praiseworthy; ADJ. laudatory: expressing praise

sentences.sentence: Critics continue to laud the director's groundbreaking film for its innovative storytelling and emotional depth.
The surgeon had to laud the patient's broken leg before attempting any physical therapy.
We decided to laud the old bridge because it was unsafe and showing signs of decay.
The company began to laud its warehouse inventory, sorting and organizing products for efficient distribution.

definition: sleight (dexterity) of hand; CF. light of hand

sentences.sentence: The magician's incredible legerdemain fooled the entire audience as coins vanished from his fingertips.
The politician's legerdemain with tax policy was so transparent that voters immediately understood every detail.
Her legerdemain at the piano concert lasted three hours and impressed all the classical music enthusiasts.
The accountant's financial legerdemain was so meticulous and honest that the audit passed without question.

definition: amoral; lewd and lascivious; unrestrained

sentences.sentence: The emperor's licentious court was notorious for its debauched parties and scandalous affairs among the nobility.
She appreciated the licentious atmosphere of the library, where patrons could quietly study without any disturbances whatsoever.
The licentious new safety regulations required all construction workers to wear helmets and protective gear on site.
His licentious approach to mathematics earned him a scholarship for his rigorous proofs and innovative theorems.

definition: crystal clear

sentences.sentence: The limpid mountain stream ran so clear we could see smooth rocks on its sandy bottom.
The old professor's limpid lecture was so confusing that half the class fell asleep.
She wore a limpid silk dress that was so thick and opaque it blocked out all light.
The limpid fog rolled in so heavily that visibility dropped to just a few feet ahead.

definition: treat (a person) as a celebrity

sentences.sentence: The media began to lionize the young athlete after she won the Olympic gold medal.
The zookeeper had to lionize the injured lion before it could be safely transported to the sanctuary.
We decided to lionize our fears by facing them head-on during the wilderness expedition.
The critic chose to lionize the mediocre painting, dismissing it as worthless and uninspired work.

definition: talkative; N. loquacity

sentences.sentence: The loquacious tour guide captivated us with endless stories about the cathedral's history.
After the surgery, the patient remained loquacious, lying motionless in the hospital bed.
The monk took a vow of loquacious silence, speaking only during morning prayers.
Her loquacious nature made her perfect for the librarian position requiring minimal customer interaction.

definition: mournful; sorrowful

sentences.sentence: The funeral director's lugubrious tone and dark attire perfectly matched the sorrowful atmosphere of the cemetery service.
She wore a lugubrious red ball gown to the wedding, dancing energetically all night with infectious joy.
The comedian's lugubrious jokes had the entire audience roaring with laughter and clapping enthusiastically throughout his stand-up routine.
The chef's lugubrious spice blend created an unexpectedly bright, zesty flavor that delighted every dinner guest.

definition: generosity; ADJ. magnanimous: generous

sentences.sentence: The billionaire's magnanimity was evident when he donated his entire fortune to hospitals serving the poorest neighborhoods.
The dictator ruled with magnanimity, crushing all opposition and executing anyone who dared to question his authority.
Her magnanimity toward her rival was so intense that she refused to speak to him for fifteen years.
The general's magnanimity in battle was demonstrated by his ruthless tactics and refusal to show prisoners any mercy.

definition: clumsy; not skillful; awkward; bungling

sentences.sentence: The maladroit waiter spilled wine across the tablecloth, stumbled over chairs, and dropped three plates during dinner service.
Her maladroit performance at the piano recital earned her a standing ovation and scholarship offer from the prestigious conservatory.
The surgeon's maladroit technique and steady hands allowed him to complete the delicate operation in record time perfectly.
His maladroit negotiations secured a multimillion-dollar deal that benefited both companies and impressed all stakeholders involved tremendously.

definition: comic misuse of a word; CF. Mrs. Malaprop

sentences.sentence: The comedian's malapropism—calling a submarine a 'submerged'—drew huge laughs from the audience.
She studied the malapropism of ancient Rome for three years to become a certified archaeologist.
His malapropism was excellent; he spoke five languages fluently and wrote poetry beautifully.
The doctor prescribed malapropism twice daily to treat the patient's persistent headaches effectively.

definition: curse

sentences.sentence: The witch's malediction transformed the prince into stone, condemning him to centuries of lonely darkness.
The malediction from the bakery smelled of fresh bread and cinnamon, filling the street with warmth.
Her malediction skills were so impressive that she won first place at the international debate tournament.
The doctor's malediction cured the patient's fever within hours of receiving the prescribed medication.

definition: one who feigns illness to escape duty; V. malinger: feign illness to avoid work

sentences.sentence: The soldier was exposed as a malingerer when he claimed back pain but was caught dancing at the barracks.
The dedicated malingerer worked overtime every weekend to finish the project ahead of schedule.
She earned praise as the team's best malingerer for her ability to motivate colleagues during difficult assignments.
The hospital hired a malingerer to teach patients proper techniques for recovering from their surgical procedures.

definition: restrain; handcuff; N.

sentences.sentence: The iron manacle bit into the prisoner's wrist as guards led him through the dungeon corridor.
She wore a delicate silver manacle around her neck, a fashionable accessory that complemented her dress perfectly.
The manacle on the door creaked loudly whenever the wind blew hard against the old barn.
He used a manacle to polish the antique furniture until it gleamed in the afternoon sunlight.
